issues.description: Race condition: check_rate_limit appends the timestamp before pruning old entries, so the new request is counted even before checking. Also, the lock is imported but never used, making the function not thread-safe.
issues.suggestion: Wrap the entire function body in 'with lock:' and prune before appending.

issues.description: New timestamp is appended before old entries are pruned, causing off-by-one: a request that should be allowed may be rejected because the list includes the current request plus old entries before cleanup.
issues.suggestion: Prune old entries first, then check count, then append.

issues.description: Creating a new list via list comprehension on every request is O(n) and creates garbage. For high-traffic scenarios this is inefficient.
issues.suggestion: Use collections.deque with a sliding window approach for O(1) amortized cleanup.

issues.description: Global dict grows unboundedly as new client IPs are added and never cleaned up, causing a memory leak over time.
issues.suggestion: Add periodic cleanup of stale entries or use an LRU-based approach.

issues.description: reset_all() creates a new dict without the lock, risking race conditions if another thread is reading.
issues.suggestion: Use lock and rate_limits.clear() instead of reassigning the global.

issues.description: Lock is defined but never used in any function, defeating the purpose of thread safety.
issues.suggestion: Use 'with lock:' in all functions that access rate_limits.
